title.en: How the EU AI Act Works — A Practical Breakdown
title.fr: Comment fonctionne l'AI Act de l'UE — guide pratique

headline.en: How the EU AI Act works
headline.fr: Comment fonctionne l'AI Act de l'UE

lede.en: The world's first comprehensive law for artificial intelligence. The trick to understanding it: it doesn't regulate the technology — it regulates what you do with it, and the rules scale with the risk.
lede.fr: La première grande loi mondiale sur l'intelligence artificielle. L'essentiel : elle ne régule pas la technologie en soi — elle régule ce que vous en faites, et les règles suivent le niveau de risque.

quiz.q: What does the EU AI Act primarily regulate?
options: How AI is used, by risk level
quiz.why: The Act is risk- and use-based: the same technology can be minimal-risk or banned depending on what it's used for.

quiz.q: A company uses AI to rank job applicants. Which tier?
quiz.why: Employment is an Annex III area, so hiring tools are high-risk and carry the heaviest set of obligations.

quiz.q: Which of these is outright banned?
quiz.why: Social scoring is one of the eight prohibited practices under Article 5 — there's no compliance path for it.

quiz.q: What's the maximum fine for using a banned practice?
quiz.why: Banned-practice breaches carry the top fine: €35M or 7% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher.

quiz.q: Your company is in the US. When does the Act apply to you?
options: When your system's output is used in the EU
Only if you have an EU office
quiz.why: The Act applies extraterritorially: if your AI's output is used in the EU, you're in scope wherever you're based.

sources.title: Where this comes from

items.label: High-level summary of the AI Act
items.note: Future of Life Institute (risk tiers, prohibited list, Annex III, GPAI duties)

items.label: European Commission — AI Act regulatory framework
Council of the EU — Digital Omnibus provisional agreement (7 May 2026)

sources.disclaimer: This is an educational explainer, not legal advice. The EU AI Act is long and evolving; specific obligations depend on facts and on guidance still being issued. Consult the official text and qualified counsel for compliance decisions.

footer: Built as a plain-language guide to Regulation (EU) 2024/1689. Facts verified against EU and Future of Life Institute primary sources, June 2026.

sections.pull: The Act is a sorting machine. Every AI system gets dropped in by its risk, and the weight of the rules matches the tier it lands in.
paragraphs: Most people expect a law that says "here are the rules for AI." The EU AI Act is built differently. It asks one question about each system — how much risk does this particular use create for people's rights and safety? — and sorts it into one of four tiers.

This is why the same piece of technology can sit in totally different tiers. A face-matching model is minimal-risk when it unlocks your phone, high-risk when it screens job applicants, and outright banned when it scrapes the open web to build a surveillance database.
sections.examplesTitle: Same tech · different use · different tier
sections.examplesLabel: Same technology, different risk tiers depending on use

examples.use: scraping the web for a DB

sections.title: The risk pyramid
sections.intro: The fewer the systems in a tier, the heavier its rules. A tiny number of uses are banned at the top; the vast majority of everyday AI sits unregulated at the bottom. Click any tier to open it.

items.name: Unacceptable — Banned
items.sub: A clear threat to rights. Prohibited outright.
body: Social scoring of people by behaviour or traits
Manipulative or exploitative systems that cause harm
Untargeted scraping of faces to build recognition databases
Emotion recognition at work or school
Most real-time facial recognition in public by police
items.note: 8 categories in total — see "The eight banned uses" below.

items.name: High risk — Regulated
items.sub: Allowed, but with heavy obligations. Most of the Act.
body: Used in hiring, credit, education, essential services, law enforcement, migration, justice…
…or as a safety part of a regulated product (medical devices, machinery, cars)
Provider must do risk management, data governance, documentation, human oversight, testing, registration
items.note: Roughly 8 sensitive areas listed in Annex III.

items.name: Limited risk — Transparency
items.sub: Just be honest that it's AI.
body: Chatbots must tell users they're talking to a machine
AI-generated images, audio and video (deepfakes) must be labelled
Synthetic content must be machine-readable as AI-generated

items.name: Minimal risk — Free
items.sub: No special obligations. The vast majority of AI.
body: Spam filters, recommendation engines, AI in video games
The bulk of AI products on the market today
items.note: Providers may still voluntarily follow codes of conduct.

callout.title: One more track, running alongside
callout.body: General-purpose AI models — the large models like GPT, Gemini or Llama that power many products — are governed by their own separate set of rules, not the pyramid. We cover those further down.

sections.title: Which tier does it land in?
sections.intro: The whole Act turns on classification. Pick a real-world AI use below and see where the sorting machine drops it — and why.

props.placeholder: ← Pick a use case to classify it.

cases.label: AI that screens and ranks job applicants
cases.why: Employment decisions are an Annex III area. Hiring tools shape people's livelihoods, so they carry the full high-risk obligations.

cases.label: A social-scoring system rating citizens by behaviour
cases.why: General-purpose social scoring by public or private actors is a prohibited practice under Article 5 — no compliance path.

cases.label: A customer-service chatbot on a website
cases.why: Allowed, but it must clearly tell users they are interacting with an AI, not a human. That's the transparency tier.

cases.label: A spam filter in your email
cases.why: No special obligations. The overwhelming majority of AI — including spam filters — sits in the free, unregulated tier.

sections.title: The eight banned uses
sections.intro: At the top of the pyramid, a short list of practices is prohibited outright across the EU — no compliance path, no exceptions for most. (Article 5.)

items.title: Manipulation & deception
items.body: Subliminal or deceptive techniques that distort behaviour and cause harm.

items.title: Exploiting vulnerabilities
items.body: Targeting age, disability or hardship to distort behaviour and cause harm.

items.title: Social scoring
items.body: Rating people by social behaviour or traits, leading to unfair treatment.

items.title: Predictive policing of individuals
items.body: Predicting crime risk based solely on profiling or personality.

items.title: Face-database scraping
items.body: Building facial-recognition databases by untargeted scraping of web/CCTV images.

items.title: Emotion recognition at work/school
items.body: Inferring emotions in workplaces or schools (bar medical/safety reasons).

items.title: Sensitive biometric categorisation
items.body: Inferring race, politics, religion, sexual orientation, etc. from biometrics.

items.title: Live public face recognition by police
items.body: Real-time remote biometric ID in public — banned but for narrow exceptions.

callout.title: The narrow exception
callout.body: Real-time facial recognition in public by police is banned by default, but narrowly allowed for things like searching for a missing child or preventing an imminent terror attack — and only with a fundamental-rights assessment and judicial sign-off.

sections.title: High-risk: where most of the law lives
sections.intro: These systems are allowed, but heavily regulated — the bulk of the Act is about them. Filter the areas, then see what providers must actually do.
sections.requirementsTitle: IF IT'S HIGH-RISK, THE PROVIDER MUST…

areas.title: Biometrics (non-banned)
areas.description: Remote identification, emotion recognition, and biometric categorisation that aren't outright prohibited.

areas.title: Critical infrastructure
areas.description: Safety components running water, gas, electricity, heating, road traffic and digital infrastructure.

areas.title: Education & training
areas.description: Deciding admissions, evaluating learning outcomes, or monitoring students during exams.

areas.title: Employment & workers
areas.description: Recruitment, filtering applications, promotion, termination, and performance monitoring.

areas.title: Essential services
areas.description: Eligibility for benefits, creditworthiness, insurance pricing, and emergency-call triage.

areas.title: Law enforcement
areas.description: Victim-risk assessment, polygraphs, evidence reliability, and profiling in investigations.

areas.title: Migration & borders
areas.description: Polygraphs, risk assessments, and examining visa or asylum applications.

areas.title: Justice & democracy
areas.description: Helping interpret and apply the law, or influencing elections and voting behaviour.

requirements.title: Risk management
requirements.body: A system to find and reduce risks across the whole lifecycle.

requirements.title: Data governance
requirements.body: Training data that's relevant, representative and as error-free as possible.

requirements.title: Technical documentation
requirements.body: Records that prove compliance to regulators.

requirements.title: Record-keeping
requirements.body: Automatic logging of events for traceability.

requirements.title: Instructions for use
requirements.body: Clear info so deployers can use it compliantly.

requirements.title: Human oversight
requirements.body: Designed so a person can intervene and override.

requirements.title: Accuracy & cybersecurity
requirements.body: Robust, accurate and secure by design.

requirements.title: Quality management
requirements.body: An organisational system to keep it all compliant.

sections.title: The parallel track: general-purpose models
sections.intro: The big foundation models don't fit the use-based pyramid — one model can be put to a thousand uses. So they get their own rules, in two layers.

columns.title: Every GPAI model
bullets: Keep technical documentation of how the model was built, trained and tested.
Give downstream developers the info they need to build on it compliantly.
Have a policy to respect EU copyright law.
Publish a summary of the training data used.
columns.note: Free & open-licence models only owe the last two — unless they hit systemic risk.

columns.title: + systemic risk
bullets: Assess and mitigate systemic risks at their source.
Track and report serious incidents to the EU AI Office.
Ensure strong cybersecurity for the model and its weights.
columns.note: These stack on top of the baseline — systemic-risk models owe both sets.

props.reason: Superscript exponent readout on a log-scale FLOP slider
props.html:
WHAT COUNTS AS "SYSTEMIC RISK"? — DRAG TO EXPLORE

The headline trigger is raw training compute. A model is presumed to pose systemic risk once the compute used to train it crosses 1025 floating-point operations (FLOP).

10²⁵FLOP of training compute
10²²10²³10²⁴10²⁵10²⁶10²⁷
Below the threshold — baseline GPAI rules apply, but not the extra systemic-risk duties.

The compute figure is the legal presumption; the Commission can also designate a model systemic on other grounds.
props.init: var slider=root.querySelector('#flopSlider');var flopVal=root.querySelector('#flopVal');var flopOut=root.querySelector('#flopOut');var sup={'0':'⁰','1':'¹','2':'²','3':'³','4':'⁴','5':'⁵','6':'⁶','7':'⁷','8':'⁸','9':'⁹','.':'.'};function toSup(n){return String(n).split('').map(function(c){return sup[c]||c;}).join('');}function upd(){var v=parseFloat(slider.value);var disp=v%1===0?String(v):v.toFixed(1);flopVal.textContent='10'+toSup(disp);if(v>=25){flopOut.className='out out-yes';flopOut.innerHTML='Systemic risk. At or above 10²⁵ FLOP, the model is presumed systemic — evaluations, red-teaming, incident reporting and cybersecurity duties all kick in.';}else{flopOut.className='out out-no';flopOut.innerHTML='Below the threshold — baseline GPAI rules apply, but not the extra systemic-risk duties.';}}ctx.on(slider,'input',upd);upd();

sections.title: What it costs to get it wrong
sections.intro: Fines are tiered to match the severity of the breach, and calculated as the higher of a fixed sum or a percentage of global annual turnover.

tiers.label: Using a banned practice
tiers.detail: The most serious breach — deploying one of the prohibited AI uses.

tiers.label: Breaking high-risk rules
tiers.detail: Most other obligation breaches — by providers or deployers of high-risk systems.

tiers.label: Giving false information
tiers.detail: Supplying incorrect or misleading information to authorities.

props.reason: Live fine calculator with whichever-is-higher logic
props.html:
DO THE MATH — THE "WHICHEVER IS HIGHER" CATCH

For SMEs and start-ups, the rule flips to whichever is lower, to keep fines proportionate.
props.init:
var turnover=root.querySelector('#turnover');var breach=root.querySelector('#breach');var calcResult=root.querySelector('#calcResult');function fmt(n){return ctx.fmtEuro(n);}function calc(){var parts=breach.value.split('-');var pct=Number(parts[0]);var fixed=Number(parts[1]);var t=Math.max(0,Number(turnover.value)||0);var pctVal=t*pct;var fixedVal=fixed;var win=Math.max(pctVal,fixedVal);calcResult.innerHTML='
'+(pct*100)+'% of turnover
'+fmt(pctVal)+'
Fixed cap
'+fmt(fixedVal)+'
Maximum fine — whichever is higher
'+fmt(win)+'
';}ctx.on(turnover,'input',calc);ctx.on(breach,'change',calc);calc();

sections.title: It arrives in waves, not all at once
sections.intro: The Act entered into force in August 2024, but its rules switch on in stages over several years — banned uses first, the heaviest high-risk duties last.

events.label: In force
events.text: The Act enters into force. Nothing applies yet — the clock starts on a staged rollout.

events.label: Live
events.text: The banned practices and AI-literacy duties become enforceable — the first rules with teeth.

events.label: Live
events.text: Rules for general-purpose AI models, the governance structure (the EU AI Office), and the penalty regime switch on.

events.label: Now-ish
events.text: Originally the date most high-risk obligations and Annex III rules applied.
events.defer: Proposed deferral: under the May 2026 Digital Omnibus, Annex III high-risk duties move to 2 December 2027.

events.label: Upcoming
events.text: High-risk AI embedded in regulated products (medical devices, machinery, vehicles) must fully comply.
events.defer: Proposed deferral: product-embedded high-risk duties may move to 2 August 2028.

events.label: Later
events.text: Remaining transition deadlines: pre-existing GPAI models, public-authority systems and large-scale EU IT systems are brought into full compliance.

sections.disclaimer: A moving target. In May 2026, EU institutions reached a provisional "Digital Omnibus" agreement to postpone the heaviest high-risk deadlines. As of this writing it is agreed but not yet formally adopted — always check the official timeline before relying on a date.

sections.title: Did it click?
sections.intro: Five quick questions. You get instant feedback and the why behind each answer.
